coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of silicon, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for enhancing enzyme activity of acid phosphatase (ACP) to dopamine and for cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuanting
Xu, Linlin
Zhao, Sheng
Miao, Chenfang
Chen, Yuyuan
Wang, Zhenzhen
Feng, Feng
Lin, Mingrui
Weng, Shaohuang
coredata.dc:description: Developing water-soluble nanomaterials with high photoluminescence emission and high yield for biological analysis and imaging is urgently needed. Herein, water-soluble blue emitting silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (abbreviated as Si-CDs) of a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 80 % were effectively prepared with high yield rate (59.1 %) via one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and trans-aconitic acid. Furthermore, the Si-CDs demonstrate environmental robustness, photo-stability and biocompatibility. Given the importance of the potentially abnormal levels of acid phosphatase (ACP) in cancer diagnosis, developing a reliable and sensitive ACP measurement method is of significance for clinical research. The Si-CDs unexpectedly promote the catalytic oxidation of ACP on dopamine (DA) to polydopamine under acidic conditions through the produced reactive oxygen species (ROS). Correspondingly, a fluorescence response strategy using Si-CDs as the dual functions of probes and promoting enzyme activity of ACP on catalyzing DA was constructed to sensitively determine ACP. The quantitative analysis of ACP displayed a linear range of 0.1–60 U/L with a detection limit of 0.056 U/L. The accurate detection of ACP was successfully achieved in human serum through recovery tests. As a satisfactory fluorescent probe, Si-CDs were successfully applied to fluorescent imaging of A549 cells in cytoplasmic with long-term and safe staining. The Si-CDs have the dual properties of outstanding fluorescent probes and auxiliary oxidase activity, indicating their great potential in multifunctional applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
High quantum yield
Promoting enzyme activity
Acid phosphatase detection
Long-term cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ACP activity in human serum samples
In vitro bioimaging of the Si-CDs
Investigation of fluorescence sensing feasibility and mechanism for ACP detection
Optimization of operating conditions and the analytical performance of detecting ACP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Mr. Zhihong Huang from the Public Technology Service Center Fujian Medical University for the technical guidance and assistance on CLSM imaging in this work. The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (No. 2021Y9007), Fujian Provincial Health Technology Project (2021GGA057), and the Startup Fund for Scientific Research, Fujian Medical University (2022QH1322).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CDs exciting at 320–390 nm. (B) The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs. (C) The TEM image and size distribution (inset) of Si-CDs. (D) The FTIR spectrum of Si-CDs. The XPS spectrum (E), and high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (F), Si (G), N1s (H), and O1s (I) of Si-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CDs, Si-CDs-DA, Si-CDs-ACP, Si-CDs-ACP-DA, and ACP-DA, respectively. (B) The UV–vis spectra of Si-CDs and the systems of ACP-DA and Si-CDs-ACP-DA at the reaction times of 30, 60 and 120 min in pH 4.0 PBS. The ESR spectra of Si-CDs, ACP, and Si-CDs-ACP with DMPO as superoxide anion scavenger (C), DMPO as ·OH scavenger (D), and TEMP as singlet oxygen scavenger (E). (F) The Stern-Volmer curve of Si-CDs-ACP-DA. (G) The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Si-CDs, Si-CDs-DA, and Si-CDs-ACP-DA. (H) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Si-CDs-ACP-DA (green) reacted for 120 min and fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs (red and blue). (I) The detected and corrected QE after IFE was eliminated in Si-CDs-ACP-DA with different concentrations of ACP. The insert of (I) is the parameters used in Formula S2, s (0.10 cm) represents the thickness of the exciting beam, d and g (0.40 cm) represent the width of the cuvette and the distance between the edge of the exciting beam and the edge of the cuvette, respectively.
The UV absorbance values at 470 nm of the ACP-DA with or without Si-CDs at the conditions of pH 4.0 (A), 5.5 (B), and 7.0 (C).

(A) Normalized fluorescence responses and corresponding photographs under ultraviolet light irradiation (insert) of Si-CDs upon adding various concentrations of ACP (0–300 U/L). (B) The values of (F0–F)/F0 of Si-CDs as a function of the concentrations of ACP. F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of ACP with constant DA, respectively. (C) The linear equation between the concentration of ACP and the value of (F0–F)/F0 of Si-CDs. (D) Normalized fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs to different interfering substances in ACP and ACP-free detection systems. (Error bars represented the standard deviation of three parallel measurements).

(A) Viability of A549 cells after incubating with 300 and 500 μg/mL of Si-CDs for 24 h. (B) Time-dependent stability of staining signals of A549 cells treated by 200, 300 and 400 μg/mL of Si-CDs. (C) Confocal microscopic and bright field images of stained A549 cells by 0, 200, 300 and 400 μg/mL Si-CDs for 24 h. The exciting wavelength of B, C was 405 nm. (Error bars represented the standard deviation of three parallel measurements).
The confocal microscopic images of A549 that were respectively labeled with 300 μg/mL of Si-CDs and 1 μM SYTO 9 for 30 min.
Schematic illustration of dual functional Si-CDs applied to ACP detection and cell imaging.

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of silicon, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for enhancing enzyme activity of acid phosphatase (ACP) to dopamine and for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Provincial Hospital, Shengli Clinical Medical College of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Provincial Governmental Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Health College, Fuzhou, 350003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuzhou University Affiliated Provincial Hospital, Fuzhou, 350001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fuzhou University Affiliated Provincial Hospital, Fuzhou, 350001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing water-soluble nanomaterials with high photoluminescence emission and high yield for biological analysis and imaging is urgently needed. Herein, water-soluble blue emitting silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (abbreviated as Si-CDs) of a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 80 % were effectively prepared with high yield rate (59.1 %) via one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and trans-aconitic acid. Furthermore, the Si-CDs demonstrate environmental robustness, photo-stability and biocompatibility. Given the importance of the potentially abnormal levels of acid phosphatase (ACP) in cancer diagnosis, developing a reliable and sensitive ACP measurement method is of significance for clinical research. The Si-CDs unexpectedly promote the catalytic oxidation of ACP on dopamine (DA) to polydopamine under acidic conditions through the produced reactive oxygen species (ROS). Correspondingly, a fluorescence response strategy using Si-CDs as the dual functions of probes and promoting enzyme activity of ACP on catalyzing DA was constructed to sensitively determine ACP. The quantitative analysis of ACP displayed a linear range of 0.1–60 U/L with a detection limit of 0.056 U/L. The accurate detection of ACP was successfully achieved in human serum through recovery tests. As a satisfactory fluorescent probe, Si-CDs were successfully applied to fluorescent imaging of A549 cells in cytoplasmic with long-term and safe staining. The Si-CDs have the dual properties of outstanding fluorescent probes and auxiliary oxidase activity, indicating their great potential in multifunctional applications.

The water-soluble blue emitting silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) with high quantum yield and high yield rate can promote the catalytic oxidation of dopamine to polydopamine by ACP and can stain cells in cytoplasmic with long-term and safe behavior.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), possessing significant features of ideal and tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity and surface modification, endow them wide range of applications in sensors, bioimaging, and other biomedical research [1,2]. It has been proven that doping heteroatoms can regulate the electronic energy levels and band gaps of CDs [3]. Heteroatom with different electronegativities, like N, S and F, can adjust the optical performance and electronic characteristics of doped CDs in modulated degrees for variable applications [4–6]. For example, the doping of N will induce the different luminescent wavelength and quantum yield of CDs [7,8]. Silicon is an extremely important element on the earth, possessing excellent optical, electronic and mechanical properties, and has received extensive attention and applications in the fields of optoelectronic, energy and bioanalysis [9–11]. Silicon and carbon are of the same main group, with lower electronegativity than carbon. The doping of silicon into CDs may introduce additional active sites for the need for sensing and cell imaging [12,13]. The expected application of Si doped CDs puts forward the requirements for synthesis, such as the desire to obtain larger-scale water-dispersed Si doped CDs with ultra-bright luminescence and controllability under low-cost and mild conditions.

Acid phosphatase (ACP) is an important phosphatase that is widely present in various mammalian tissues and agricultural fields, playing a crucial role in regulating various cellular functions [14,15]. In clinical practice, as an important tumor marker of metastatic prostate cancer, the determination of ACP activity has been used for the diagnosis of cancer and monitoring of cell viability [16–18]. Therefore, establishing a reliable and sensitive ACP detection method is significant. At present, the developed ACP detection methods are mainly based on ACP's enzymatic hydrolysis of specific substrates to release specific products and regulate signals [19]. Recently, our group reported that dopamine (DA) is oxidized to polydopamine under ACP catalysis and used for detecting ACP under neutral conditions [20]. However, the issue is that the sensitivity of measuring ACP is insufficient [20] ascribed to two main factors. Firstly, the limited ability of ACP to catalyze DA to generate pDA is insufficient to increase the sufficient amount of pDA generated by ACP catalysis. Secondly, the quantum yield of the fluorescent probe is not high enough, resulting in a less significant change in its signal amplitude [21,22]. Correspondingly, we envision introducing auxiliary tools in the measurement system to improve the efficiency of enzymatic catalyzation of DA, and applying probes with excellent luminescence performance to improve the measurement performance of ACP.

The attention to fabricating nanomaterials as enzyme mimics is increasing [23–25]. The design of nanomaterials as enzyme mimics has markedly boosted biomedical developments [25,26]. Correspondingly, for biological enzymes, the use of nanomaterials to promote enzyme activity or stabilize enzyme activity has also been gradually developed [27]. For example, the appropriate application of hollow double hydroxides or covalent organic frameworks can effectively encapsulate enzymes and maintain or even enhance the enzyme's biological activity in specific environments [27,28]. So far, silicon-based nanomaterials with enzymatic activity or enhanced enzymatic reactions are mainly silica nanomaterials [29,30] and porous silica nanocomposites loaded with other nanomaterials [31–33]. However, a certain nanomaterial that promotes enzyme catalyzed reactions and establishes detection methods based on its own luminescent properties has not been reported.

Inspired by the doping of N for the improvement of carbon dots and the potential effect of Si for carbon dots, herein, we successfully synthesized water-soluble blue emitting silicon and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (abbreviated as Si-CDs) with high stable fluorescence quantum yield (80 %) via one-step hydrothermal reaction by using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and trans-Aconitic acid as raw material. And the yield rate was 59.1 %. It is interesting to note that Si-CDs can enhance the efficiency of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production during the ACP enzymatically catalyzing DA oxidation to polydopamine under acidic conditions, thereby improving the efficiency of producing polydopamine (Scheme 1 ). Using Si-CDs as probe, a fluorescence response strategy based on ACP catalyzing DA was constructed to sensitively determine ACP. As a satisfactory fluorescent probe, Si-CDs were successfully applied to long-term imaging of A549 cells targeting cytoplasmic with satisfactory biosafety (Scheme 1). This work foreshadows the design and development of CDs with specific and excellent photophysical and chemical performance, which is expected to enhance the multifunctional application potential of CDs.

N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO), trans-Aconitic acid, dopamine (DA), Acid phosphatase (ACP), Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), Tyrosinase (TYR), Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), xanthine oxidase (XOD), Glycose Oxidase (GOD), Trypsin, Lysozyme, Glucosidase, Glycine (Gly), ascorbic acid (AA), Glucose (Glu), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Adrenaline, Isoproterenol, Levodopa, NaF were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). SYTO9 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Shanghai) was utilized for nuclear staining in living cells.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Carry Eclipse, USA). UV–visible absorption spectrum was scanned by UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2450, Japan). The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) were collected on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet iS50, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained by transmission electron microscope (Thermo Talos F200S, USA). The atomic force microscope (AFM) was operated on Dimension Icon (Bruker, German) using ScanAsyst mode. Xenon lamp (Niubit Technology Co., Ltd HSX-F/UV300, Beijing) was used to conduct the anti-light bleaching test. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) was used to measure the chemical composition and chemical state of Si-CDs. The Powder X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, German) was used to perform phase analysis of Si-CDs. The cell viability was determined by microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, Finland) at 490 nm. The imaging and distribution of Si-CDs in A549 cells were captured by using the TCS-SP8 confocal microscopy (Leica, Germany) with a laser operated at 405 nm.

Originally, 0.1741 g of trans-Aconitic acid was dissolved in 18 mL of water and saturated with nitrogen for about 10 min to remove dissolved oxygen. Then 2 mL of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) was added into the above solution under continuous stirring for 20 min. The precursor solution was transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling, the prepared yellow product was purified with a cut-off dialysis bag (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 48 h to remove excess raw materials and possible by-products. The purified product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Different concentrations of ACP, 20 μL Si-CDs (25 μg/mL) and 6 μL DA (300 μM) were mixed with 10 mM PBS (pH 4.0) in EP tube. The total volume of the testing solution in EP tube was controlled at 200 μL. The mixture was reacted at 25 °C for 100 min. Then, the fluorescence spectrum was collected between 380 nm and 600 nm with exciting at 370 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set to 5 nm. The F and F0 were the fluorescence values of Si-CDs-DA at 450 nm in the presence and absence of ACP. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE, (F0–F)/F0) was applied as a quantitative standard. The Si-CDs-DA, Si-CDs-ACP, ACP-DA, and Si-CDs-ACP-DA represented the mixed solutions of Si-CDs and DA, Si-CDs and ACP, ACP and DA, and Si-CDs, ACP and DA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ACP activity in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of serum samples, 4 blood samples were acquired from healthy volunteers at the Fujian Provincial Hospital. After the centrifugation of blood samples at 8000 rpm for 10 min, 20 μL Si-CDs (25 μg/mL), 300 μM DA and different specific concentrations (5 U/L, 10 U/L) of ACP were added into the above serum samples diluted with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 4.0) to determine the spiked recovery of ACP according to the same procedure as ACP activity assay.

Various amounts of NaF (1–100 mM) as an inhibitor were added into the EP tubes containing 20 μL Si-CDs (25 μg/mL), 300 μM DA, 200 U/L ACP and PBS (10 mM, pH = 4.0) with the total volume of the testing solution was made to be 200 μL. Fluorescence spectra were scanned after the mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 100 min. The inhibition efficiency (IE) of NaF was calculated as shown below:
(Equation 1)
I E =
F inhibitor
−
F noinhibitor
F 0
−
F noinhibitor
× 100 ％
where
F inhibitor
and
F noinhibitor
refer to the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs-ACP-DA in the presence and absence of inhibitor, respectively.
F 0
is the fluorescence intensity of the Si-CDs-DA system in the absence of ACP and inhibitor. The half maximum inhibition value (IC50) was given from the graph of the inhibition efficiency of NaF as a function of its concentration.

The cytotoxicity of Si-CDs was determined by standard MTT assay. Human non-small-cell lung cancer cells (A549) were inoculated in 96-well plates at a density of 4*103 cells/well. After culturing for 24 h, the RPMI medium mixing with 100 μL different amounts of Si-CDs (0, 300, 500 μg/mL) was added to the cell culture. After culturing for 24 h, the cells were incubated with a medium containing 0.5 mg/mL MTT for 4 h. Finally, 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used to replace the medium. After shaking the 96-well plates at room temperature for 15 min, the optical density value was obtained at 490 nm by spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bioimaging of the Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the imaging ability of Si-CDs to live cells, A549 cells were inoculated in a 24-well plate for 24 h. After washing with PBS (pH = 7.4) three times, the cells were incubated in RPMI-1640 medium containing different concentrations (200, 300, and 400 μg/mL) of Si-CDs at 37 °C for different times (0.5, 2, 12, 24 h) in 5 % CO2. The fluorescence imaging of the cells was obtained using a confocal microscope with 405 nm laser excitation. To observe the persistence of staining of Si-CDs on the cells, the cells were incubated with Si-CDs of different concentrations as mentioned above, and then washed with PBS. The medium with Si-CDs was further cultured for 24 h, and then subjected to confocal imaging. The double staining method was used to determine the Si-CDs staining area of cells. The cells were first labeled with Si-CDs (300 μg/mL) and then the A549 cells were labeled again with a specific nuclear dye SYTO9 in the same manner as above. The staining region of Si-CDs was determined by merging the two pictures.

The optical properties and structures of Si-CDs were examined. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the Si-CDs exhibited stable fluorescence emission centered at 450 nm under excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 390 nm, indicating that Si-CDs displayed photoluminescence (PL) behavior unrelated to the exciting wavelengths. Furthermore, the fluorescence of Si-CDs exhibited the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 nm and 450 nm. And the full width at half maximum (FWHM) was 70 nm (Fig. 1B). The Si-CDs displayed characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks at 206 nm and 350 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* transition of CC (the conjugated sp2 core) and the n-π* transition of CO (the carbonyl/carboxyl) [33]. The relative quantum yield (QY) of Si-CDs was 80 % by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (Fig. S1A). TEM image illustrated that the average diameter of Si-CDs was 3.52 nm and they were quasi-spherical particles (Fig. 1C). The size distribution was close to normal distribution. As shown in Fig. S1B, the AFM image revealed the morphologic heights of Si-CDs in the range of 1.5 nm–7.5 nm with an average height of 4.19 nm, indicating that the CDs primarily consisted of one or a few layers of graphene-like plates [34]. The AFM result was roughly consistent with the TEM results. The elemental composition and functional groups of Si-CDs were verified by FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1D, the FTIR absorbance peak at 2932 cm−1 matched with the C–H symmetric stretching vibration, while the characteristic peaks at 3420 and 1580 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of the N–H/O–H and the bending vibration of the N–H, respectively. The peaks located at 693 and 781 cm−1 implied the existence of the secondary amine and N–H wagging vibration. The Si–O–Si bonding was reflected by the sharp absorbance peaks at 1110 and 1020 cm−1 [35]. Additionally, the absorption peak at 928 cm−1 was ascribed to Si–O vibration in the Si–O–H group. The amide bond vibration at 1658 cm−1 and 1316 cm−1 demonstrated the OC–N bonds formed due to the bonding between the amino from DAMO and carboxyl groups from trans-aconitic acid. The XPS spectrum showed mainly four typical peaks at 102.33, 284. 8, 398.84 and 532.03 eV, attributed to Si, C, N, and O, respectively (Fig. 1E). The atomic contents of Si-CDs were 14.23 % (Si), 48.45 % (C), 13.64 % (N) and 23.68 % (O). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1F) displayed three fitted peaks at 284.6, 285.8, and 287.6 eV belong to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CO, respectively [36,37]. The Si2p XPS spectra (Fig. 1G) exhibited three fitting peaks at 101.1, 102.1, and 102.7 eV, corresponding to Si–C, Si–N, and Si–O, respectively [38]. The three fitted peaks at 398.2, 399.0, and 399.8 eV of the N1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1H) corresponded to pyridine N, amino N, and pyrrole N, respectively. The O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1I) showed two fitted peaks that were associated with CO (531.9 eV) and C–O/O–H (532.1 eV), respectively [39]. The XPS results showed that the Si-CDs possessed multiple chemical groups of C–C (or C–H), Si–O, C–NH2, CO and C–N–C. In particular, the amide bonds may be derived from the coupling reaction of silane molecules with trans-aconitic acid in the synthetic process, which was consistent with FTIR. Both the FTIR results and XPS information indicated that Si-CDs were successfully doped with Si and N elements, and the surface was rich in functional groups like CO, Si–O, and C–NH2. These heteroatom-doping and multifunctional groups endowed Si-CDs with high fluorescence QY, excellent water solubility, and biocompatibility [7,12,13].

The XRD pattern of Si-CDs in Fig. S1C illustrated a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.5°, corresponding to nearly amorphous nature of Si-CDs, which was consistent with TEM [40,41]. It is worth noting that the intensities of potential three standard diffraction peaks of Si (0) at ≈28.5° (111), ≈47.5° (220), and ≈56.5° (311) were very low, indicating the low content of crystalline silicon [42,43]. In order to assess the hydrophilicity of Si-CDs, the dispersion in water and organic solvents was compared. Water, chloroform and ethyl acetate were applied to compare the dispersion of Si-CDs in solvents. The Si-CDs were separately dissolved in a mixture of chloroform and water, ethyl acetate and water (Fig. S2). As illustrated in Fig. S2, the Si-CDs were uniformly dispersed in water, with not only ultra-bright blue emission under 365 nm irradiation but also visible to the white light, owning to the ultra-high fluorescence and excellent water solubility of Si-CDs.

The synthetic yield of this strategy for Si-CDs was evaluated. After heating with 0.1741 g trans-Aconitic acid and 2.06 g DAMO in a high-pressure polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, the purified product was dried to be 1.3195 g with a synthesis yield of up to 59.1 % (Figs. S3A and B), which was higher than most reported methods of Si-CDs (Table S1). The Si-CDs powder showed pale yellow color in sunlight and bright blue emission under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Figs. S3C and D). In general, the existing synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials that meet high yields and QY was rare. The vast majority can only meet one of the conditions or both were less than 50 % (Table S1). In this work, no complicated modification process is required, instead, a simple mixing of the raw materials under a nitrogen atmosphere. After high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, highly fluorescent Si-CDs with considerable synthetic yield can be obtained by purification.

The optical stability of Si-CDs was investigated. The normalized PL intensity of Si-CDs remained relatively constant at variable pH from 4 to 12 (Fig. S4A). Similarly, Si-CDs were incubated at different temperatures for 15 min (Fig. S4B), the PL intensities only increased slightly at high temperatures (75–95 °C), indicating that the Si-CDs had robust pH stability and high-temperature resistance. In addition, variable concentrations of NaCl played little impact on the luminous intensities of Si-CDs (Fig. S4C), demonstrating the outstanding stability of Si-CDs in the ionic environment. The long-term storage durability of Si-CDs was indicated by the fact that their PL intensity barely diminished after 90 days of room temperature storage (Fig. S4D). Furthermore, compared with the two almost bleached commercial dyes (FAM and ROX) after 4 h of continuous irradiation using a 300 W xenon lamp at a wavelength of 350 nm, the PL intensities of the Si-CDs remained fairly stable (Fig. S4E). Additionally, after continuous excitation at 350 nm for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs remained above 99.8 % (insert of Fig. S4E). This indicated that Si-CDs exhibited excellent anti-bleaching ability. Furthermore, the Zeta potentials of Si-CDs in buffer with different pH values were examined. As shown in Fig. S4F, the Zeta potential of Si-CDs was 0.8 mV at pH 4 condition. The Zeta potentials of Si-CDs stabilized around −1.3 mV between pH 5–8, and then decreased to −3.0 mV between pH 9–12, indicating the presence of weak deprotonation effect with increasing pH value. These findings implied that the Si-CDs behaved with exceptional stability in the varied settings, implying the promising application prospects of Si-CDs bottomed on the coexisting benefits of simple preparation process and low cost.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of fluorescence sensing feasibility and mechanism for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the response of Si-CDs for detecting ACP based on ACP catalyzed DA was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs were stable with DA or ACP. The mixture of ACP and DA (ACP-DA) behaved negligibly background fluorescence response. Nevertheless, when mixed Si-CDs with ACP-DA (Si-CDs-ACP-DA), Si-CDs was remarkably quenched. The results indicated that DA could interact with ACP and eventually induce fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs.

The quenching mechanism of ACP with DA as a substrate on Si-CDs was investigated. First, the interaction relationship among Si-CDs, ACP, and DA was discussed. It has been reported that DA is easily oxidized and undergoes polymerization under appropriate conditions, such as self-polymerization by dissolved oxygen oxidation under alkaline conditions [44,45] and H2O2-mediated polymerization under the action of mimic peroxidase [46]. As shown in Fig. 2B, the UV–vis spectra of the ACP-DA were recorded at different times (30, 60, 120 min) under the condition of pH 4.0. Among them, there was only one absorption peak at 280 nm, which belonged to DA. There was no absorption in the range of 300 nm–700 nm. After introducing Si-CDs, the Si-CDs-ACP-DA not only retained the original absorption peak of DA at 280 nm, but also showed the shoulder peak at 310 nm (belonged to the early oxidation product of DA pigment) and the wide absorption peak centered at 472 nm (belonged to polydopamine oligomer). The absorption values of the Si-CDs-ACP-DA at 310 nm and 472 nm gradually increased with the prolongation of reaction time, which meant that the formation of DA pigment and polydopamine oligomer increased. The above results showed that DA was difficult to oxidize and polymerize under the condition of pH 4.0 catalyzed by ACP, while the Si-CDs-ACP could induce the polymerization of DA to form polydopamine oligomer under this condition. Considering the influence of pH on the polymerization of DA [20], the effect of pH on the catalyzed oxidation of DA with the coexistence of Si-CDs and ACP was further investigated. Usually, the activity of ACP needs weakly acidic conditions. While, there was barely any oxidation at pH 4.0 (Fig. 2B), implying the little effect of ACP on DA oxidation under acidic conditions. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3 A–C, as the pH values increased from 4.0 to 7.0, the absorption values of ACP-catalyzed oxidation of DA at 470 nm gradually increased, which was in accord with the oxidation of DA by ACP at neutral condition [20]. While the degree of reaction of ACP catalyzed DA and the difference in absorbance with ACP-DA by introducing Si-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of pH (Fig. 3A–C), indicating that Si-CDs significantly promoted ACP-catalyzed oxidation of DA to produce polydopamine in pH 4.0. Since dissolved oxygen can be activated to form oxidative species with nano-catalyst [47,48], the possibly formed ROS for the oxidation of DA to polydopamine oligomers were evaluated in this detecting system containing Si-CDs and ACP (Fig. 2C–E). Little electron spin resonance (ESR) signal was observed in simple Si-CDs or ACP with different spin trapping agents, suggesting that there was no ROS production. While, the characteristic ESR signal peaks of superoxide anions (O2 ˙-), hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2) were observed in the mixture of Si-CDs and ACP using scavengers [49,50]. These results showed that the mixture of Si-CDs and ACP possessed the catalytic ability to decompose dissolved oxygen to generate ROS of O2 ˙-, ∙OH, and 1O2. Although the detailed process of the mixture of Si-CDs and ACP catalyzing the decomposition of dissolved oxygen to generate ROS is still unclear, these observations demonstrate the formation of polydopamine oligomers in this system is caused by ROS. It has also been confirmed that Si-CDs can promote the enzymatic oxidation of DA by ACP to form polydopamine.

Second, the quenching mechanism of Si-CDs by polydopamine oligomers produced by Si-CDs-ACP catalyzed oxidation of DA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2F, the Stern-Volmer curve exhibited a good linear relationship by using ACP concentration as the horizontal axis according to Formula S1 in the Si-CDs-ACP-DA system, indicating the presence of static quenching effects (SQE) or dynamic quenching effects (DQE) in the quenching system [33,51]. Simultaneously, no new absorption peaks appeared in this quenching system except for the absorption peak of polydopamine oligomers (Fig. 2B), which indicated that there were no non-fluorescent complexes formed in this quenching system, suggesting the little effect of SQE. Moreover, the comparison of the fluorescence lifetime decays of the Si-CDs-ACP-DA was conducted. As shown in Fig. 2G, the fluorescence lifetimes of Si-CDs, Si-CDs-DA, and Si-CDs-ACP-DA, as measured by time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, were found to be 13.9 ns, 14.1 ns, and 12.8 ns, respectively. Notably, the lifetime of Si-CDs remained unaltered upon the introduction of DA, whereas a significant alteration was observed upon the introduction of ACP-DA, suggesting the strong interaction between Si-CDs and ACP-DA. This observation suggested that DQE was responsible for the quenching [51]. Additionally, the polydopamine oligomers formed from DA catalyzed by Si-CDs-ACP had a wide absorption spectrum centered at 472 nm, which overlapped with the emission spectrum of Si-CDs (Fig. 2H). Therefore, it was believed that the quenching mechanism of Si-CDs by polydopamine oligomers possibly originated from the inner filter effect (IFE). Correspondingly, the QE correction was performed based on the geometric parameters (insert of Fig. 2I) and Formula S2 [52]. As demonstrated in Fig. 2I and Table S2, the corrected and observed QE of Si-CDs varied at each concentration of ACP. The spectral overlap and difference in corrected and observed QE indicated the existence of IFE interaction between Si-CDs and ACP-DA. The above results showed the quenching mechanism of ACP-DA on Si-CDs was impacted by the dual effects of DQE and IFE. Combined with the promoting enzymatic oxidation of Si-CDs to ACP for formed polydopamine, Si-CDs played the other role of fluorescent probe for ACP detection using DA as substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of operating conditions and the analytical performance of detecting ACP
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions of the Si-CDs-ACP-DA system were optimized by comparing the quenching efficiency (QE, (F0–F)/F0) of the Si-CDs. In accordance with the discussion of the detecting mechanism, a pH of 4.0 was chosen as the appropriated pH condition. Correspondingly, the kinds of buffers for controlling pH were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5A, the largest QE was acquired in PBS, suggesting the most appropriate buffer system of PBS for ACP activity. Moreover, the effects of temperature and time were investigated (Fig. S5B). As common temperatures for the biosensing, 25, 30, and 37 °C were selected for optimization. The QE steadily increased as the reaction time was delayed. When the action time reached 100 min, the QE at 25 °C was the highest. Considering the time cost, 25 °C and 100 min were selected as the most ideal reaction temperature and time. Moreover, the concentration of the substance of DA was optimized. In the detection system of Si-CDs-ACP-DA, as the concentration of DA raised to 300 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the Si-CDs dropped sharply (Figs. S5C–D), while it kept constant between 300 μM and 500 μM. Therefore, 300 μM DA was set as the optimal substrate concentration for ACP detection.

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs in the detecting system were monitored upon various concentrations of ACP (CACP). As shown in Fig. 4 A, Si-CDs were gradually quenched and accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence illustration under ultraviolet light irradiation (insert of Fig. 4A) with the increase of CACP. The QE was gradually enhanced (Fig. 4B). A linear relationship was observed between QE and the concentration of ACP within a range of 0.1–60 U/L (Fig. 4C). The linear equation was described as y = 0.0083CACP + 0.0130 (R2 = 0.9975). The limit of detection was 0.056 U/L according to 3σ/S (σ was the SD of blank values (n = 9); S was the slope of the linear equation). Compared to other developed strategies (Table S3), this strategy illustrated the features of low detection limit and wide linear range. The expression level of ACP is usually 0–9.0 U/L in healthy human serum, while, the level will increase in pathological conditions, like prostate disease [53]. The performance implied the suitability of this assay for ACP detection in clinical routine. In addition, the specificity and selectivity of the assay to ACP were assessed. As shown in Fig. 4D, the common interferent caused little signal changes to the detecting system without ACP, which were similar to the blank solution without ACP, suggesting the specificity of the developed method. Furthermore, the interference did not cause apparent signal changes in the process of detecting ACP (Fig. 4D), implying the selectivity of this strategy. The results indicated that the system owned highly specific enzymatic catalysis to DA using Si-CDs as probe, while other biological proteins or small biomolecules did not hold the ability to promote DA polymerization. The adding standard content of ACP in human serum was detected to evaluate the application prospect. As shown in Table S4, the spiked recovery ranged from 90.5 % to 107.8 % for known concentrations of ACP in diluted serum, and the relative standard deviation varied from 1.24 % to 7.04 %, all of which meant the developed ACP assay possessed significant potential in clinical analysis.

Furthermore, the possibility of this method for screening potential ACP inhibitors was examined. NaF, a typical ACP inhibitor [54], was selected as the model to evaluate the screening ability of this Si-CDs-ACP-DA system for inhibitors. As shown in Fig. S6, with the increased concentrations of NaF, the inhibition efficiencies (IE) calculated by Equation (1) increased gradually. The inhibition may be due to that the generating ROS from the system of Si-CDs and ACP was inhibited by the introduced NaF. Correspondingly, the half-maximal inhibition value (IC50) was determined to be 5.38 mM. The results also demonstrated that the Si-CDs-ACP-DA system could be employed to screen ACP inhibitors during drug delivery.

The cell imaging potential of Si-CDs was investigated. First, the cytotoxicity of Si-CDs was verified by the MTT method. Using A549 as the model cell, the 24-h co-culture of Si-CDs and cells suggested that the cell viability was greater than 90 % with different and high concentrations of Si-CDs (Fig. 5 A), suggesting the good biosafety of Si-CDs.

By culturing A549 cells in various concentrations of Si-CDs at different times, the images were captured using a confocal microscope under excitation at 405 nm. Compared with the corresponding bright field images (Fig. S7), Fig. 5B showed that the cells displayed strong blue fluorescence after staining for 30 min. And the blue fluorescence increased with the extension of the action time, suggesting the effective staining ability of Si-CDs in cells. The fluorescence reached its strongest when incubated for 24 h. At the same time, it was found the shape of A549 cells did not change with the treatment of long time and high concentration of Si-CDs, confirming the biocompatibility of Si-CDs. In order to investigate the possibility of continuous staining and long-term imaging of A549 cells by Si-CDs, the A549 cells incubated with Si-CDs for 24 h at the above concentrations were washed with PBS and continued to culture in the medium for 24 h without Si-CDs, as shown in Fig. 5C. After 24 h incubation with various concentrations of Si-CDs, the Si-CDs were still present in the cells and sent out strong and stable fluorescent signals, suggesting that the staining stability of Si-CDs in cells. Simultaneously, cells maintained good morphology from the bright field images (Fig. 5C), ascribed to the biocompatibility of the Si-CDs. These results suggested the great potential of Si-CDs for long-term cell imaging with the advantage that Si-CDs were not easily removed by cell metabolism. The double staining of STYO9 and Si-CDs was operated to determine the staining area of Si-CDs in cells (Fig. 6 ). After 30 min of staining, the regions labeled with Si-CDs were mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm, while the nuclei were mainly labeled with STYO9. The results indicated that the Si-CDs exhibited good cytoplasmic targeting ability.

In conclusion, we have developed a straightforward yet highly effective one-pot bottom-up hydrothermal method for the preparation of fluorescent Si-CDs by utilizing DAMO and trans-aconitic acid as source. Remarkably, the prepared Si-CDs also exhibited strong photoluminescence behavior with the QY of up to 80 %, alongside excellent water dispersibility, robust stability and outstanding biocompatibility. Notably, the Si-CDs illustrated the extraordinary feature of promoting enzyme activity of ACP to catalyze DA to polydopamine under acidic conditions. Furthermore, the fluorescence analysis strategy for ACP detection was successfully established with excellent performance. Cell imaging experiments further demonstrated that Si-CDs were suitable for long-term biological imaging. In short, Si-CDs synthesized in high yield are conducive to multifunctional applications, such as in vivo and in vitro imaging and biosensors attributed to excellent optical properties and enhancing enzyme activity.

Yuanting Chen: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linlin Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sheng Zhao: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Chenfang Miao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Yuyuan Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhenzhen Wang: Data curation. Feng Feng: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Mingrui Lin: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Shaohuang Weng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Mr. Zhihong Huang from the Public Technology Service Center Fujian Medical University for the technical guidance and assistance on CLSM imaging in this work. The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (No. 2021Y9007), Fujian Provincial Health Technology Project (2021GGA057), and the Startup Fund for Scientific Research, Fujian Medical University (2022QH1322).
